title: Business Climate change study that overestimated economic impact is pulled — but not before banks relied on it By Taylor Herzlich Published Dec
raw_response: {"rating": 1.6, "lean": "Lean Right", "explanation": "This is a news report. The author’s framing emphasizes that a climate economics study \"overestimated\" impacts and that central banks relied on it before retraction, casting skepticism on climate-risk policymaking; this points rightward. Limited balancing prose (calling the revised impact \"still a big blow\") tempers the tilt, resulting in a Lean Right rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) Headline frames the paper as overestimating damage and stresses that banks relied on it before it was pulled, implying institutional credulity toward climate-risk narratives. (2) The lede reiterates that Nature retracted a paper that \"overestimated\" the toll, again tying this to central banks’ scenario-building, a critical framing of climate-focused financial oversight. (3) The author labels the 62% figure a \"faulty number\" and notes it was \"roughly three times typical estimates,\" a dismissive characterization consistent with right-lean skepticism of high-end climate projections. (4) The piece highlights that the number was used by policymakers and the NGFS to update scenarios, underscoring a chain of policy uptake based on flawed research without providing any authorial defense of those institutions’ processes. (5) While the author notes the corrected estimate would still be a \"big blow,\" the overall selection and emphasis primarily criticize overstatement and institutional reliance, producing a modest rightward lean."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report. The author’s framing emphasizes that a climate economics study "overestimated" impacts and that central banks relied on it before retraction, casting skepticism on climate-risk policymaking; this points rightward. Limited balancing prose (calling the revised impact "still a big blow") tempers the tilt, resulting in a Lean Right rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) Headline frames the paper as overestimating damage and stresses that banks relied on it before it was pulled, implying institutional credulity toward climate-risk narratives. (2) The lede reiterates that Nature retracted a paper that "overestimated" the toll, again tying this to central banks’ scenario-building, a critical framing of climate-focused financial oversight. (3) The author labels the 62% figure a "faulty number" and notes it was "roughly three times typical estimates," a dismissive characterization consistent with right-lean skepticism of high-end climate projections. (4) The piece highlights that the number was used by policymakers and the NGFS to update scenarios, underscoring a chain of policy uptake based on flawed research without providing any authorial defense of those institutions’ processes. (5) While the author notes the corrected estimate would still be a "big blow," the overall selection and emphasis primarily criticize overstatement and institutional reliance, producing a modest rightward lean.
